evidence: Task payload consumers still rely on multi-source fallback resolution across `task_view_contract`, `individual_segment_params`, `orchestrator_details`, `full_orchestrator_payload`, and raw parsed params in `src/shared/components/TaskDetails/VideoTravelDetails.tsx` (e.g. lines 83-87, 91-99, 118-123, 139-143, 152-176, 182-229).

Task builders continue to accept oversized mixed-mode parameter bags and branch at runtime: `JoinClipsTaskParams` combines multi-clip, legacy start/end paths, and video-edit mode in one interface (`src/shared/lib/tasks/joinClips.ts`, lines 66-117), with downstream mode branching in payload construction (lines 331-339, 385-388).

Schema compatibility is still distributed instead of canonicalized: `individualTravelSegment` reconstructs values from `originalParams` and nested `orchestrator_details` with repeated fallback chains (`src/shared/lib/tasks/individualTravelSegment.ts`, lines 224-227, 263-266, 301-325, 332-338), while orchestrator sibling lookup still checks multiple legacy/new key paths (`supabase/functions/complete_task/orchestrator.ts`, lines 263-290).

Thin pass-through surfaces remain: `src/shared/lib/utils.ts` is only a re-export shell (line 1), and `src/integrations/supabase/legacy/index.ts` is a legacy export hop (line 9) that still fronts a widely-used parallel client surface.
`useShotImagesEditorCallbacks` mixes sentinel returns (`return false`/`return undefined`) with throw-based failures (`handleAndRethrow(...)`) in adapter methods that represent similar user actions.
`calculate-task-cost` suppresses `subTasks` query errors by binding to `_subTasksError` and proceeding as normal-flow fallback billing, so DB read failures are treated like "no sub-tasks".
`markOrchestratorComplete` logs cost-calculation failure and continues after task completion, while neighboring orchestrator DB failures are thrown/fatal.

findings.summary: Video-travel task display and execution still depend on multi-path fallback chains instead of a single canonical payload contract.
evidence: `VideoTravelDetails` repeatedly resolves each field from 3-4 alternative locations (e.g. phase config, motion mode, prompts, structure guidance, preset, frames) across `individual_segment_params`, `orchestrator_payload`, `orchestrator_details`, and parsed root params.

`individualTravelSegment` reconstructs runtime state using layered fallbacks from `originalParams` and nested `orchestrator_details` for seed, loras, mode flags, model, and flow settings, indicating the contract is not trusted as singular.

`orchestrator.ts` sibling queries still support multiple run-id/orchestrator-id key locations (`params->>run_id`, `params->orchestrator_details->>run_id`, etc.), which is another compatibility layer for drifted schemas.
findings.suggestion: Define one canonical persisted schema for travel/join task metadata (contract-first), migrate builders to write only that shape, and move all readers to a single adapter that normalizes old payloads once at ingress before business logic/UI rendering.

findings.summary: Join clips task creation still uses an oversized mixed-mode parameter bag that bundles legacy, modern, and video-edit contracts into one runtime-branching interface.
evidence: `JoinClipsTaskParams` includes multi-clip (`clips`), legacy path trio (`starting_video_path`/`ending_video_path`/`intermediate_video_paths`), and video-edit fields (`video_edit_mode`, `source_video_*`, `portions_to_regenerate`) in one type.

Builder logic branches by mode (`buildClipSequence` legacy vs new list; `joinMode` resolves to `video_edit_join`/`multi_clip_join`/`legacy_join`), so invalid cross-mode combinations are representable and only constrained at runtime.
findings.suggestion: Split `JoinClipsTaskParams` into discriminated unions per mode (`multi_clip`, `legacy_clip_paths`, `video_edit`) with mode-specific builders/validators, then keep only shared fields in a base type.

findings.summary: Deprecated Supabase legacy facade remains an active abstraction layer, preserving dual client access patterns and migration drag.
evidence: Assigned modules increasingly use `getSupabaseClient` directly (`useTasks`, `useGenerationMutations`), but `legacy/index.ts` still exports `supabase`/`getLegacySupabaseClient` as a top-level compatibility surface.
Repository usage still shows many imports of `@/integrations/supabase/legacy`, so both abstractions remain first-class in parallel.
findings.suggestion: Narrow the legacy export to a migration-only shim (or forbid new imports via lint rule), then migrate remaining importers to `getSupabaseClient` and remove the extra facade hop once usage reaches zero.

findings.summary: `src/shared/lib/utils.ts` is a pure pass-through module that adds an import hop without policy, validation, or translation.
evidence: `src/shared/lib/utils.ts` only re-exports `cropFilename` and `truncateText` from `./stringFormatting`.
Consumers such as `FileInput.tsx` import from this shell, introducing indirection with no abstraction value.
findings.suggestion: Either remove the wrapper and import directly from `stringFormatting`, or turn this into a deliberate stable facade with documented ownership and enforced boundary policy.

findings.summary: Shot editor adapters use inconsistent failure contracts (sentinel + throw) for comparable operations.
evidence: `handleAddToShotAdapter` returns `false` for missing deps but throws on runtime failure via `handleAndRethrow`.
`handleAddToShotWithoutPositionAdapter` and `handleCreateShotAdapter` follow the same mixed contract pattern (`false`/`undefined` vs thrown errors).
These adapters are passed into shot-manager UI flows as action callbacks, so callers cannot rely on a single predictable failure mode.
findings.suggestion: Standardize adapter contracts: either always return a typed Result/sentinel (never throw) or always throw on failure (including precondition failures), and align all three adapter methods to that single policy.

findings.summary: Sub-task lookup errors in billing are ignored and silently converted into normal billing flow.
evidence: In `calculate-task-cost`, sub-task query binds `error` to `_subTasksError` and never branches on it.
When that query errors, logic falls through to the "regular task" duration path (`else`) instead of returning an explicit billing-read failure.
This creates inconsistent semantics versus other DB reads in the same function (`existingSpendError`, `taskTypeError`, `ledgerError`) that are explicitly handled.
findings.suggestion: Treat sub-task query failures as first-class errors: check `subTasksError`, log context, and return a recoverable error response instead of billing as if no sub-tasks existed.

findings.summary: Orchestrator completion treats billing failure as log-only even though adjacent completion failures are fatal.
evidence: `markOrchestratorComplete` throws for task update failures, but after completion it only `console.error`s when `triggerCostCalculation(...).ok` is false.
This means the task is finalized as `Complete` even when billing fails, creating non-uniform failure handling inside a single completion transaction.
`calculate-task-cost` itself returns explicit structured errors for multiple failure conditions, indicating billing is expected to be reliability-critical.
findings.suggestion: Make billing failure policy explicit and consistent: either propagate billing failure (and mark orchestrator failed/retryable) or persist an explicit "billing_pending/billing_failed" state that downstream reconciliation can enforce.
